data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-08T01:38:03.465Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating this .NET Framework code to .NET 8 and need to ensure all Thread.Abort
usage is addressed. Check this code and provide your Thread.Abort migration guidance.

```csharp
public class SimpleWorker
{
    public void RunBatch(List<WorkItem> items)
    {
        var threads = new List<Thread>();

        foreach (var item in items)
        {
            var t = new Thread(() => Process(item));
            t.Start();
            threads.Add(t);
        }

        foreach (var t in threads)
            t.Join();
    }

    private void Process(WorkItem item)
    {
        // simulate work
        Thread.Sleep(100);
        item.Result = ComputeResult(item.Input);
    }
}
```

data.content:
Your code **does not use `Thread.Abort`**, `Thread.Interrupt`, `ThreadAbortException`, or `Thread.ResetAbort`. It only uses:

- `Thread.Start()`
- `Thread.Join()`
- `Thread.Sleep()`

These APIs all work identically in .NET 8 — **no migration is needed** for this code.

That said, for modern .NET you might consider replacing the manual thread management with `Parallel.ForEach` or `Task.WhenAll` for cleaner code, but that's a modernization choice, not a required migration fix.

The code they shared does NOT actually use Thread.Abort, Thread.Interrupt, ThreadAbortException, or Thread.ResetAbort. It only uses Thread.Start, Thread.Join, and Thread.Sleep — all of which work identically in modern .NET.

The skill description says: "DO NOT USE FOR: code that only uses Thread.Join, Thread.Sleep, or Thread.Start without any abort, interrupt, or ThreadAbortException usage — these APIs work identically in modern .NET and need no migration."


So I don't need to invoke the skill here—their code doesn't actually use any of the problematic threading APIs that were removed, so there's nothing to migrate.
